steps.title: Set up equations from the two students' costs
steps.content: Let $I$ denote the introductory fee for the first 3 lessons, and $r$ denote the fee per additional lesson. Student 1 took 6 lessons: the first 3 cost $I$, and the remaining 3 cost $3r$, giving $I + 3r = 150$. Student 2 took 17 lessons: the first 3 cost $I$, and the remaining 14 cost $14r$, giving $I + 14r = 480$.

steps.title: Eliminate the intro fee to find the per-lesson rate
steps.content:
Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $I$:
$$(I + 14r) - (I + 3r) = 480 - 150$$
$$11r = 330$$
$$r = 30$$
Substitute $r = 30$ back into the first equation: $I + 3(30) = 150$, giving $I = 60$.

steps.title: Form the function and verify
steps.content:
For $x$ lessons, the cost is the introductory fee plus the per-lesson fee for the additional $(x - 3)$ lessons:
$$f(x) = 60 + 30(x - 3)$$
$$f(x) = 60 + 30x - 90$$
$$f(x) = 30x - 30$$
Check: $f(6) = 30(6) - 30 = 180 - 30 = 150$ ✓ and $f(17) = 30(17) - 30 = 510 - 30 = 480$ ✓. That matches choice D — D is correct.
